clean_story: War on a board.  Sixty-four squares, infinite battles. "Chess" comes from shah, Persian for "king"—because the game’s heart is a silent duel to trap yours. It’s like commanding an army where every soldier moves differently, and the general can’t shout orders. If it’s correct to see chess as a test of wit, its hidden layer is geometry. A knight’s L-shape isn’t random—it’s the only path that lets it jump over pawns like a frog over lily pads. Bishops slice diagonally, turning the board into a star of intersecting lasers. The queen? A rook and bishop fused, wielding cross-shaped power. One rule makes it brutal:No luck. Every loss is a lesson carved by your own hands. In 1997, IBM’s Deep Blue beat world champion Garry Kasparov—not by creativity, but by calculating 200 million moves per second. Yet humans still outplay computers in "bullet chess," where each side gets 60 seconds total. Why?Instinct trumps math when time collapses. Three related subjects are game theory, cognitive training and go rules.
